A.latin: miles, qui gladium tenet, fortis est.
A.translation: The soldier, who is holding a sword, is brave.
notes: accept 'the soldier who holds a sword is brave' (no commas also acceptable)

A.latin: regina, quae in urbe habitat, pulcherrima est.
A.translation: The queen, who lives in the city, is very beautiful.
notes: accept 'the queen who lives in the city is most beautiful'

A.latin: puella, quam pater amat, in silva est.
A.translation: The girl, whom the father loves, is in the wood.
notes: accept 'the girl whom her father loves is in the wood'

A.latin: dei, quos milites oppugnaverunt, iam discesserunt.
A.translation: The gods, whom the soldiers attacked, have now departed.
notes: accept 'the gods whom the soldiers attacked have already left'

A.latin: comes, cuius vultus tristis erat, nihil dixit.
A.translation: The companion, whose face was sad, said nothing.
notes: accept 'the companion, whose expression was sad, said nothing'

A.latin: dona, quae rex accepit, pulchra erant.
A.translation: The gifts, which the king received, were beautiful.
notes: accept 'the gifts (which) the king received were beautiful'

A.latin: silva, in qua puella dormit, ingens est.
A.translation: The wood, in which the girl is sleeping, is huge.
notes: 'in qua' = in which (prep. + rel. pronoun f.abl.sg)
accept 'the wood where the girl is sleeping is huge'

A.latin: iter, quod dux constituerat, facillimum fuit.
A.translation: The journey, which the leader had decided, was very easy.
notes: 'quod' n.acc.sg = which (object, neuter) — NOT 'because' here (rel. pronoun)

A.latin: omnes milites, qui coacti erant, tandem discesserunt.
A.translation: All the soldiers, who had been forced, finally departed.
notes: 'coacti erant' pluperfect passive of 'cogo' — 'had been forced' / 'had been gathered'

A.latin: verbum, quod maritus dixit, tristissimum erat.
A.translation: The word, which the husband said, was very sad.

A.latin: uxor, quam maritus amabat, cibum statim coepit accipere.
A.translation: The wife, whom the husband loved, immediately began to receive food.

A.latin: filii, quibus pater persuaserat, statim festinaverunt.
A.translation: The sons, whom the father had persuaded, immediately hurried.
notes: 'quibus' dative pl = to whom / whom (dative because 'persuadeo + dat')
accept 'the sons, whom the father had persuaded, hurried at once'

B.translation: The leader arrived in order to gather the soldiers.
notes: 'ut + pres subj' = purpose clause — 'in order to' / 'so that he might' / 'to'
accept 'so that he could gather the soldiers'
'advenit' here perfect (sequence of tenses with present subj is 'informal' at GCSE — accept 'arrives')

B.latin: pater domum festinavit ut filium videret.
B.translation: The father hurried home in order to see his son.
notes: accept 'so that he might see his son' / 'so that he could see his son'

B.latin: regina verba dixit ut deos oraret.
B.translation: The queen spoke words in order to beg the gods.
notes: accept 'so that she might pray to the gods' / 'so that she could pray to the gods'

B.latin: milites gladios tenuerunt ut urbem oppugnarent.
B.translation: The soldiers held swords in order to attack the city.
notes: accept 'so that they might attack the city' / 'so that they could attack the city'

B.latin: puella clamabat ut pater statim adveniret.
B.translation: The girl was shouting in order that her father might arrive immediately.
notes: accept 'so that her father might arrive at once' / 'so that her father would come at once'

B.latin: comites ad silvam iverunt ut iter facerent.
B.translation: The companions went to the wood in order to make a journey.
notes: accept 'so that they might travel' / 'so that they could go on a journey'

B.translation: The goddess appeared in order to persuade the queen.
notes: accept 'so that she might persuade the queen' / 'so that she could persuade the queen'

B.latin: uxor maritum oravit ut domum rediret.
B.translation: The wife begged her husband to return home.
notes: 'oravit ut + subj' — 'begged (him) to'; the 'ut' here leans towards an indirect command at GCSE — accept 'begged him that he would return home' / 'begged her husband to return home'
accept 'pleaded with her husband to come back home'

B.translation: The son shouted in order that his father might hear.
notes: accept 'so that his father might hear him' / 'so that his father could hear'

B.translation: The leader said (an order) that the soldiers should escape.
notes: 'dico + ut + subj' = indirect command — 'told them to X'; preferred natural: 'The leader told the soldiers to escape'
accept 'The leader said that the soldiers should flee' / 'The leader said (something) so that the soldiers might escape'

B.translation: It was necessary that the king should depart.
notes: 'necesse erat ut + subj' = it was necessary that X should Y
accept 'the king had to leave' / 'it was necessary for the king to depart'

B.latin: maritus diu in urbe habitavit ut uxorem videret.
B.translation: The husband lived in the city for a long time in order to see his wife.
notes: 'diu' = for a long time / a long while
accept 'so that he might see his wife' / 'so that he could see his wife'

C.latin: cum milites advenissent, rex laetus fuit.
C.translation: When the soldiers had arrived, the king was happy.
notes: accept 'since the soldiers had arrived' / 'after the soldiers had arrived'

C.translation: When the goddess was begging, everyone heard.
notes: 'oraret' imperf subj — accept 'while the goddess was begging'

C.latin: cum pater clamaret, filii statim festinaverunt.
C.translation: When the father was shouting, the sons immediately hurried.
notes: accept 'since the father was shouting' / 'while the father was shouting'

C.latin: cum hic rex verba dixisset, omnes discesserunt.
C.translation: When this king had said the words, everyone departed.
notes: accept 'after this king had spoken the words' / 'since this king had said the words'

C.latin: cum uxor dormiret, maritus domum rediit.
C.translation: While the wife was sleeping, the husband returned home.
notes: accept 'when the wife was sleeping' / 'since the wife was sleeping'

C.latin: cum dux iter facilius fecisset, milites coepit cogere.
C.translation: When the leader had made a fairly easy journey, he began to gather the soldiers.
notes: 'facilius' comparative of 'facile' (adv) or 'facilis' (n.sg) — here adjectival with 'iter': 'a fairly/quite easy journey' / 'a rather easy journey' / 'an easier journey'

C.translation: When they had finally arrived, they opened the gate.
notes: accept 'since they had finally arrived' / 'after they had finally arrived'

C.latin: cum tam iratus esset, dux nihil dixit.
C.translation: Since he was so angry, the leader said nothing.
notes: accept 'when he was so angry, the leader said nothing' / 'because he was so angry...'

C.latin: cum mortem cupivisset, tandem effugere non potuit.
C.translation: Since he had desired death, he was finally not able to escape.
notes: 'tandem' = at last / finally / in the end
accept 'although he had desired death' — wait: cum+subj doesn't mean 'although'; do NOT accept 'although' here
accept 'when he had wanted death, he could not finally escape' / 'since he had wanted to die...'

C.latin: cum puella tristis esset, pater eam amavit.
C.translation: Since the girl was sad, her father loved her.
notes: accept 'when the girl was sad' / 'because the girl was sad'

C.latin: cum omnes comites redissent, dux cibum accepit.
C.translation: When all the companions had returned, the leader received food.
notes: accept 'after all the companions had returned' / 'since all the companions had returned'

C.latin: cum verba audita essent, dei discesserunt.
C.translation: When the words had been heard, the gods departed.
notes: accept 'after the words had been heard' / 'since the words had been heard'

D.latin: si dux milites cogeret, statim discederent.
D.translation: If the leader were gathering the soldiers, they would immediately depart.
notes: present contrary-to-fact: 'si + imperf subj ... + imperf subj' — 'if X were -ing, Y would -'
accept 'if the leader forced the soldiers, they would leave at once'

D.translation: If you had arrived, you would have seen the/your son.
notes: past contrary-to-fact: 'si + pluperf subj ... + pluperf subj' — 'if X had -ed, Y would have -ed'
accept 'if you had come, you would have seen your son'

D.latin: si pater non clamaret, uxor dormiret.
D.translation: If the father were not shouting, the wife would be sleeping.
notes: accept 'if the father did not shout, the wife would sleep'

D.latin: si regina verba audivisset, tandem effugisset.
D.translation: If the queen had heard the words, she would have finally escaped.
notes: accept 'had listened to the words' / 'had heard the words'

D.latin: si necesse esset, milites domum redirent.
D.translation: If it were necessary, the soldiers would return home.
notes: accept 'if it were necessary, the soldiers would go back home' / 'would come back home'

D.latin: si dea oraret, omnes deos audirent.
D.translation: If the goddess were begging, all would hear the gods.
notes: accept 'would listen to the gods' for 'deos audirent'

D.latin: si hoc donum rex accepisset, laetus fuisset.
D.translation: If the king had received this gift, he would have been happy.

D.latin: si puella clamaret, pater statim adveniret.
D.translation: If the girl were shouting, her father would immediately arrive.
notes: accept 'would come at once' / 'would reach her at once' for 'statim adveniret'

D.latin: si tam iratus esset, nihil diceret.
D.translation: If he were so angry, he would say nothing.

D.latin: si iter facilius fuisset, milites discessissent.
D.translation: If the journey had been easier, the soldiers would have departed.
notes: accept 'would have left' / 'would have gone away' for 'discessissent'

D.latin: si comes rediisset, omnes cibum accepissent.
D.translation: If the companion had returned, everyone would have received food.

D.latin: si ceteri effugere possent, solus dux nihil diceret.
D.translation: If the rest were able to escape, the leader alone would say nothing.
notes: 'solus' = alone / only — 'the leader alone' / 'the lone leader' / 'only the leader'
accept 'if the others could flee, only the leader would say nothing'
